sp.p: (aside) She's a tigress when she's up ; I know her of old. Well, Mrs. R., I came to offer you ten pounds on account; here it is. (offering bank notes)

sp.speaker: Mrs. R.
sp.p: If there's any thing in those boxes worth ten pound, I'll risk it, and keep them as they are till I get my forty-three pound five shilling.

sp.speaker: Biles.
sp.p: You're acting illegally; allow me to explain the law.

sp.speaker: Mrs. R.
sp.p: Do ! and I'll explain possession—which is nine points of it!

sp.speaker: Biles.
sp.p: I'll give you twenty pounds if you'll allow me to open one of them.

sp.speaker: Mrs. R.
sp.p: Now I'm sure they're worth my money : forty-three pound five shillings.

sp.speaker: Biles.
sp.p: Now, Mrs. R., you're a woman of uncommon sense, and know there's no getting blood out of a stone.

sp.speaker: Mrs. R.
sp.p: My common sense tells me that if the stone will bleed at all, it will bleed a good deal. Forty-three pound five shillings, if you please, Mr. Stone.

sp.speaker: Biles.
Mrs. R.
sp.p: Clarkson Biles is a nice article to make so many bones about paying a poor lone widow her due, when he'd got it in his pocket all the time. I'll give you your receipt as you come down: you must make haste, for my lodger may be coming back directly.

sp.speaker: Biles.
sp.p: Let him come ; Biles knows how to apologize like a gentleman. Now, you be off!

sp.speaker: Mrs. R.
sp.p: (aside, as she goes out) Well, this is a windfall; I never expected to get this. Exit door in flat.

sp.speaker: Biles.
sp.p: (R.) All goes on swimmingly; but I must be cautious —as cunning as a fox ; that fellow Scumley is a queer customer to deal with, and if he were once to get this will in his hands before I get my share of the estate secured, I might whistle for my chance; lor' bless me, he'd destroy it in a moment; but I haven't allowed him to get the smallest scent of the place where I've got the document, and I'll make it secure enough now. Now for it. (takes out a key and opens the box) Here's a lot of rubbish ! (bundling out papers) It was a lucky thing for me that I didn't destroy that will—I don't know why the deuce I didn't —and it was a lucky thing for me I saved this identical box from the fire; I don't know why the deuce I did : it was fate! Fate had predestined Biles to be a capitalist—where the devil is it ? I thought it was at the top—no, it must be at the bottom; Mother Raby has been tossing this box about; everything's reversed.

div.stage: Enter Scumley, silently, door in flat—leans over the back of arm chair, C., and watches.

sp.speaker: Biles.
sp.p: Ha, ha! if that rascal Scumley had only known where I had it, he'd have been here before me, and –– Oh ! what do you want here?

sp.speaker: Scum.
sp.p: (C) Why, considering that you got fifty pounds out of me, and not having much opinion of the honesty or honour of the world in general, I kept my eye upon you ; in other words, I dodged you to this house, and not seeing you come out of it, I introduced myself to the landlady as your elder brother, and the good soul sent me up to assist you.

sp.speaker: Biles.
sp.p: (R.) Well, then, you may go down again; the will's not here.

sp.speaker: Scum.
sp.p: What are all those papers?

sp.speaker: Biles.
sp.p: Nothing at all to do with your business ; be kind enough to leave me: I can't attend to you just now.

sp.speaker: Scum.
sp.p:
Well, I'm glad to see there's some appearance of your
not having deceived me. The will is amongst those papers ; now don't deny it, because you'll only force me to come and search them myself.

sp.speaker: Biles.
sp.p: (barricading himself with the table and chairs) If you come near, I'll open the window and shout for the constabulary. (takes his keys and quickly opens the other box) Look here—yes, this is what I wanted. (takes out a large horse pistol) This is what we used to keep in our strong room at the office; I used to sleep there when there was any property to protect. I know it's charged, I loaded it myself four years ago. Now I'll look over the papers, but you keep where you are, or I shall be compelled to pick you off. Do we understand each other?

sp.speaker: Scum.
sp.p: Do you take me for a rogue ?

sp.speaker: Biles.
sp.p: I see we understand each other, so I'll go on with my job. (searches) I've examined those—not there, nor there. (taking all out) 'Tisn't here! it's gone—gone—gone !

sp.speaker: Scum.
sp.p: What's that ?

sp.speaker: Biles.
sp.p: It's not here, I tell you !

sp.speaker: Scum.
sp.p: So you said before. None of your tricks upon me, or I'll–

sp.speaker: Biles.
sp.p: Can't you see I am in earnest ? I'm robbed—I'm robbed! it's gone!

sp.speaker: Scum.
sp.p: (leaps over the table and seizes him) You scoundrel! will you dare to play upon me? I'll–

sp.speaker: Biles.
sp.p: Don't, don't! I'm in earnest; I—I can't find it—I'm ruined ! Give me time to think ; I'll swear it was there.

sp.speaker: Scum.
sp.p: I'll look myself. (searching papers)

sp.speaker: Biles.
sp.p: I hope you may find it. (a pause)

sp.speaker: Scum.
sp.p: No—no

div.stage: Enter Miriam, door in flat—-Biles seeing her utters an exclamation ; rushes across and places himself between Miriam and the door, as she is about to retreat.

sp.speaker: Miriam.
sp.p: (C) What are you doing in my apartment ?

sp.speaker: Biles.
sp.p: (up, l.) Ah! you hear? Her apartment—she has it; she has taken it!

sp.speaker: Scum.
sp.p: (R.) Bolt the door! (Biles bolts it) Have you opened this box ?

sp.speaker: Miriam.
sp.p: Why should you think so ?

sp.speaker: Scum.
sp.p: Have you opened this box ?

sp.speaker: Miriam.
sp.p: Why, sir—do—you—ask?

sp.speaker: Scum.
sp.p: Oh, prevarication ! You have, it's plain–

sp.speaker: Biles.
sp.p: As the nose on my face.

sp.speaker: Scum.
sp.p: Where's the paper you took out of it ?

sp.speaker: Biles.
sp.p: Where is my paper that you've stolen ?

sp.speaker: Scum.
sp.p: Give it up !

sp.speaker: Biles.
sp.p: To me! to me, mind! Give it me, or––

sp.speaker: Miriam.
sp.p: You terrify me ; I haven't got it.

sp.speaker: Scum.
sp.p: I see by your manner you have taken it. I am not a man to be trifled with ! (takes up the pistol) Give up that paper this instant, or I'll blow you into eternity!

sp.speaker: Miriam.
sp.p: (falling on her knees) Don't murder me!

sp.speaker: Biles.
sp.p: Search her, search her ; search everywhere ! (they each seize her, she screams)

sp.speaker: Bernard.
sp.p: (without) Open the door!

sp.speaker: Biles.
sp.p: Who's that? (Miriam again screams, the door is broken open)

div.stage: Enter Bernard, followed by Huffin—Bernard rushes to Scumley and throws him off— Huffin tackles Biles—Mrs. Raby appears at back.

sp.speaker: Bernard.
sp.p: You cowardly vagabonds!

sp.speaker: Mrs. R.
sp.p: (at back, C.) What a disreputable scene in a repu­table house.

sp.speaker: Scum.
sp.p: (L.) She has robbed this man ; picked his lock.

sp.speaker: Biles.
sp.p: (C) I had a will in that box, which I intended to take this morning to Mr. Reynolds, for I knew he'd reward me handsomely; and my friend there got exasperated when he found the girl had taken it away, and it was all that I could do to protect her from his violence.

sp.speaker: Huff.
sp.p: (R. C.) You expect me to believe that ? You'd better call me a fool at once.

sp.speaker: Scum.
sp.p: (aside) There's a lying rascal; I'll owe him a turn. (aloud) I believe that will to have been a forgery.

sp.speaker: Huff.
sp.p: I must be a fool if I can't swear to my own signature.

sp.speaker: Biles.
sp.p: Oh, then you have got it ?

sp.speaker: Huff.
sp.p: Yes, sir, Mr. Reynolds met me and placed it in my hands; and your friend may get back to Norfolk Island as soon as he likes.

sp.speaker: Scum.
sp.p: Nothing of the sort; I'm no canting ticket-of-leave-man. I've served my time, and my character is now unimpeachable, you old fool!

sp.speaker: Huff.
sp.p: He's done it—he's called me a fool at once!

sp.speaker: Mrs. R.
sp.p: (R. C.) About this will—who comes into the property then ?

sp.speaker: Biles.
sp.p: (C) What's that to you? Keep your place, woman.

sp.speaker: Huff.
sp.p: (crossing to Biles) This lady will be my mother, fellow, and I'll thank you to address her respectfully.

sp.speaker: Bernard.
sp.p: (L C.) Oh! indeed ?

div.stage: Exit Mrs. Raby. with dignity, door in flat.

sp.speaker: Scum.
sp.p: (L) I'll have this will affair thoroughly looked into.

sp.speaker: Huff.
sp.p: We'll take care of that.

sp.speaker: Biles.
sp.p:
Yes, we'll take care of that. I can prove how and
when I saved it from the flames on purpose to serve Mr. Bernard, knowing he would reward me liberally.

sp.speaker: Huff.
sp.p: Hold your tongue, sir.

sp.speaker: Scum.
sp.p: Come I don't want to put you to expense in contesting this; if Mr. Reynolds will give me three thousand, I'll relinquish all claim.

sp.speaker: Huff.
sp.p: And I his lawyer! you'd better call me a—oh, I forgot; you did. Not a penny piece will you get from us, you unprincipled disgrace to the family. You have no claim.

sp.speaker: Biles.
sp.p: My learned brother is right; you have no claim.

sp.speaker: Scum.
sp.p: I shall claim the right of knocking you down, if you interfere between me and my relations. You have swindled me out of fifty pounds. (Biles gets behind the door)

sp.speaker: Huff.
sp.p: Which you raised on certain chattels from Mrs. Wilson's estate, and for which, if you shew your nose there again, we shall claim restitution.

sp.speaker: Scum.
sp.p: I shall go and place my case in the hands of some respectable solicitor.

sp.speaker: Huff.
sp.p: Do, some Old Bailey lawyer.

sp.speaker: Scum.
sp.p: I feel that I am wasting my time in talking to such an old ass. At any rate, if my half-nephew does not behave handsomely, I'll publish our relationship, and my antecedents; what d'ye say to that ? Good day. (as Scumley is going out, he sees Biles through a crevice in the door, which opens on to the stage, he returns and gives him a blow on the head—exit Scumley, door in flat)

sp.speaker: Biles.
sp.p: He's gone; I've much to be thankful for.

sp.speaker: Huff.
sp.p: Miss West, I must ask your pardon for my suspicions.

sp.speaker: Miriam.
sp.p: No, sir; all seemed to confirm them.

sp.speaker: Huff.
sp.p: Possibly, Mrs. Wilson might have made a will in your favour.

sp.speaker: Biles.
sp.p: That she did; my late employer, Mr. Scrivener, drew it out, and I was one of the witnesses ; I hope you'll remember that fact; a little liberality on your parts would be the making of Biles.

sp.speaker: Bernard.
sp.p: I know you to be a rogue; but still as this circumstance has proved so fortunate, I shan't forget you.

sp.speaker: Huff.
sp.p: Nor must you forget to do something for this girl; she must not be cast upon the world without a shilling.

sp.speaker: Bernard.
sp.p: I owe all to her; and if she will accept the love. —for I do love her now, of one, who will no longer be the worthless unthrift he has been, it will be the festival day of my life, when, as my wife, she consents to share with me the property, which has been so unexpectedly recovered.

sp.speaker: Huff.
sp.p: Stop, stop, stop ! I hope that Miriam has too much good sense to allow you to marry her.

sp.speaker: Miriam.
sp.p: Let no gratitude for questionable heroism be mistaken, Bernard, for love ; if I ever on false grounds, indulged in a hope, I have bitterly felt the humiliation and punishment. Live wisely and happily, and all that is past will be forgotten in the one joy of seeing that accomplished for which I rashly ventured so much.

sp.speaker: Bernard.
sp.p: Dear Miriam, the humiliation is mine; but as sure as I believe in the solemn record of a vow—I love you. I can have no happiness unless you consent to be mine.

sp.speaker: Huff.
sp.p: She won't, she won't; or I know nothing of her character.

sp.speaker: Miriam.
sp.p: If I again err, may forgiveness follow, as it has done my other faults. Bernard, I do consent.

sp.speaker: Huff.
sp.p: You do ? Then you've made a fool of me at once. (to Bernard) Upon my word, I must question the respectability of your connexion: decided dark coloured sheep. Your putative uncle is a returned convict, and your wife will be a lady who does not hesitate to destroy a will–

sp.speaker: Biles.
sp.p: Or pick a lock. Excuse me; it's only our fun. Biles and his learned brother (pointing to Huffin) are humorous fellows, but the right sort.

sp.speaker: Bernard.
sp.p: Miriam's were innocent crimes, and bless her for them..

sp.speaker: Miriam.
sp.p: Will all look upon me with the lenient eye that you do ? (to Audience) Can a right motive justify a wrong act? Dare you say "no !" you, who in your kindness so over slow to censure—so ever quick to encourage, give such an example? Our immortal bard has taught how few of us can escape in the mere course of justice. Mercy we look for— and at your hands.

div.stage: Huffin. Miriam. Bernard. Biles. r. l.
